summary: This lesson continues practicing stem-changing verbs (e→ie, o→ue) and introduces the 'we' form (-amos) for -ar verbs. It explains that the 'we' form does not trigger stem changes because the stress falls on the ending, not the stem. Arabic loanwords in Spanish are also discussed.

teachings.conceptName: Stem change: e splits to ie under stress
teachings.summary: Practiced the e→ie split with querer and perder. Not all e's split — there's no way to know in advance, but most of the time it will happen.

teachings.conceptName: Stem change: o splits to ue under stress
teachings.summary: Practiced with colgar (to hang). Not all o's split (comer → como, no split). There's no way to predict — you experiment and get feedback.
teachings.notes: Always go back to the infinitive to determine the correct ending — colgar is an -ar verb, so he/she form is cuelga, not cuelge.

teachings.summary: To form the 'we' version of -ar verbs in present tense, remove -ar and add -amos. The accent falls on the -a- of -amos (penultimate syllable), which is in a different position than other forms.
teachings.rule: For -ar verbs: remove -ar, add -amos to get the 'we' form.

teachings.summary: The 'we' form does not trigger stem-vowel splitting because the stress moves to the ending (-amos), away from the stem vowel. The stem vowel is no longer stressed, so it stays intact.
teachings.rule: Stem vowels (e, o) do not split in the 'we' form because they are unstressed.
teachings.notes: The stem-changing issue is fundamentally about stress — only stressed stem vowels split.

teachings.summary: Spanish words beginning with al- typically come from Arabic, where al- means 'the'. Spanish has many more Arabic loanwords than English due to 800 years of Muslim presence in southern Spain. Sometimes the 'l' in al- assimilates to match the next consonant.
teachings.rule: Words beginning al- in Spanish are almost always from Arabic, where al- means 'the'.

examples.english: rice (Arabic: al-ruz, l assimilated to r)

teachings.notes: In Arabic, the 'l' of 'al' assimilates to match the next letter for about half the consonants. This is why azúcar (not alzucar) and arroz (not alroz). Spanish never has double-s (posible, pasión), but does have double-r (arroz, perro).

teachings.summary: Pensar means to think, related to 'pensive'. It stem-changes e→ie. 'Pensar hacer' (to think to do) means to plan on doing.

examples.english: I think so (I think that yes)
What do you plan on doing?

teachings.notes: Related to English 'pensive'. 'Pienso que sí' = I think so (literally 'I think that yes').

teachings.summary: Cerrar means to close. It stem-changes e→ie in stressed forms.

vocabulary.derivation: Arabic al-ruz, l assimilated to rr
